# Grafex

> Grafex is an open-source developer tool for programmatic image composition. Write JSX/TSX compositions with full CSS support, export to PNG. Uses a real browser engine (WebKit) — not a CSS subset. No browser window, no server, no ceremony.

Grafex works as both a CLI tool (`npx grafex export`) and a Node.js library (`import { render } from 'grafex'`). Compositions are plain TSX files that export a function component and a config object. The tool transpiles JSX via esbuild, renders HTML in a headless WebKit browser via Playwright, and screenshots the result to PNG.

Key features: full CSS support (flexbox, grid, gradients, shadows, transforms), custom fonts via config.fonts (Google Fonts or any CSS URL), AI-friendly (compositions are easy for LLMs to write), sub-100ms warm renders, 2 runtime dependencies (playwright-core + esbuild).

---

## Getting Started

Go from zero to a rendered PNG in under 5 minutes.

### Install

```bash
npm install grafex
```

Grafex uses WebKit for rendering. The browser binary is downloaded automatically during `npm install` via a postinstall script — no extra steps needed.

Requirements: Node.js >= 20.0.0

### Write a composition

Create a file called `card.tsx`:

```tsx
import type { CompositionConfig } from 'grafex';

export const config: CompositionConfig = {
  width: 1200,
  height: 630,
};

export default function Card() {
  return (
    <div
      style={{
        width: '100%',
        height: '100%',
        background: 'linear-gradient(135deg, #667eea, #764ba2)',
        display: 'flex',
        alignItems: 'center',
        justifyContent: 'center',
        color: 'white',
        fontSize: '64px',
        fontWeight: 'bold',
        fontFamily: 'system-ui, sans-serif',
      }}
    >
      Hello, Grafex!
    </div>
  );
}
```

A composition has two parts:
1. Default export — a function that returns JSX. This is your image layout.
2. Named `config` export — a `CompositionConfig` object that sets the output dimensions and optionally loads custom fonts.

### Export

```bash
npx grafex export -f card.tsx -o card.png
```

---

## Compositions

Compositions are TSX files that describe your image layout. They use a custom JSX runtime — not React — so there are no hooks, no state, and no effects. Just pure functions that return markup.

### CompositionConfig

```ts
interface CompositionConfig {
  width?: number;   // Image width in pixels (default: 1200)
  height?: number;  // Image height in pixels (default: 630)
  format?: 'png';   // Output format (only PNG supported)
  fonts?: string[]; // URLs to font stylesheets (e.g., Google Fonts)
}
```

### Custom Fonts

Load custom fonts via `config.fonts` with Google Fonts or any CSS font URL:

```tsx
export const config: CompositionConfig = {
  width: 1200,
  height: 630,
  fonts: [
    'https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Playfair+Display:wght@700&display=swap',
  ],
};

export default function Card() {
  return (
    <div style={{ fontFamily: "'Playfair Display', serif", fontSize: '48px' }}>
      Custom Font Text
    </div>
  );
}
```

### Styling

Style compositions using the `style` prop with a CSS-in-JS object. Unlike Satori, Grafex supports the full CSS spec because it renders in a real browser engine — flexbox, grid, calc(), CSS variables, z-index, gradients, shadows, transforms all work.

### Props

Compositions can accept props for dynamic content:

```tsx
interface Props {
  title: string;
  date: string;
}

export default function BlogCard({ title, date }: Props) {
  return (
    <div style={{ padding: '80px', background: '#0f172a', color: 'white' }}>
      <div style={{ fontSize: '48px', fontWeight: 'bold' }}>{title}</div>
      <div style={{ fontSize: '20px', color: '#94a3b8', marginTop: '16px' }}>{date}</div>
    </div>
  );
}
```

Pass props via CLI: `grafex export -f card.tsx --props '{"title":"My Post","date":"March 2026"}'`
Pass props via API: `render('./card.tsx', { props: { title: 'My Post' } })`

### Custom Components

Compositions can import components from other files. Grafex bundles everything with esbuild before rendering.

---

## CLI Reference

### `grafex export`

Render a composition file to a PNG image.

```bash
grafex export -f <file> [options]
```

| Flag | Short | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `--file` | `-f` | string | — | Path to the `.tsx` composition file (required) |
| `--out` | `-o` | string | `./output.png` | Output file path |
| `--props` | | string (JSON) | `{}` | Props to pass to the composition |
| `--width` | | number | from config | Override composition width in pixels |
| `--height` | | number | from config | Override composition height in pixels |
| `--format` | | string | `png` | Output format (only `png` supported) |
| `--help` | `-h` | | | Show help text |

Examples:

```bash
grafex export -f card.tsx -o card.png
grafex export -f card.tsx -o card.png --props '{"title":"Hello"}'
grafex export -f card.tsx -o card.png --width 800 --height 400
```

Exit codes: 0 = success, 1 = error.

### Global Flags

```bash
grafex --version    # Print version and exit
grafex --help       # Print help text and exit
```

---

## Library API

Use Grafex programmatically in Node.js applications.

```ts
import { render, close } from 'grafex';
```

### `render(compositionPath, options?)`

```ts
const result = await render('./card.tsx', {
  props: { title: 'Hello' },
  width: 1200,
  height: 630,
});
// result.buffer is a Buffer containing PNG data
```

Returns `Promise<RenderResult>`:

```ts
interface RenderResult {
  buffer: Buffer;
  width: number;
  height: number;
  format: 'png';
}
```

### `close()`

Shut down the browser process. Always call this when done rendering.

```ts
await close();
```

### Batch rendering

The browser is reused across `render()` calls — warm renders are 5-50ms:

```ts
for (const page of pages) {
  const result = await render(page.file);
  writeFileSync(page.out, result.buffer);
}
await close();
```

### Advanced Exports

```ts
import { h, Fragment, renderToHTML, BrowserManager } from 'grafex';
```

### Types

```ts
interface RenderOptions {
  props?: Record<string, unknown>;
  width?: number;
  height?: number;
  browser?: 'webkit';
}

interface CompositionConfig {
  width?: number;
  height?: number;
  format?: 'png';
  fonts?: string[];
}
```

---

## Browser Setup

Grafex renders using headless WebKit via Playwright.

### Automatic Installation

`npm install grafex` downloads WebKit automatically via postinstall.

### Manual Installation

```bash
npx playwright-core install webkit
```

### PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH

Override browser cache location:

```bash
export PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH=/path/to/browsers
npx playwright-core install webkit
```

### CI Setup (GitHub Actions)

```yaml
steps:
  - uses: actions/checkout@v4
  - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
    with:
      node-version: 20
  - run: npm ci
  - run: npx playwright install-deps webkit
  - run: npx playwright-core install webkit
  - run: npx grafex export -f card.tsx -o card.png
```

### Troubleshooting

- "Browser not found": Run `npx playwright-core install webkit`
- "Missing system dependencies" (Linux): Run `npx playwright install-deps webkit`
- Slow first render: Normal — browser launches once, subsequent renders are 5-50ms
- Disk space: WebKit is ~2MB cached (vs Chromium 200MB+)
